docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Limbo, The City

Everything ends up here eventually. Lost ships, lost souls, lost gods. We're the silt in the drain of the multiverse. Once you wind up here, there's no leaving -- so you'd best pick up a gun and get to work before someone else flatlines you.

Limbo is a high-lethality tactical tabletop RPG set in a recursive pocket dimension suspended somewhere in the void between dimensions. It tells the stories of the billions of denizens of The City, all trapped in its looping space. Search for power, meaning, and belonging in a place where violence is an everyday language and life is as meaningless as the rest of the waste around you.

docs.title: The Setting: The City of Scraps
docs.text:
You are trapped in The City. A construct known as The Heart generates a bubble of habitable reality within the crushing nothingness of the Void. It pulls in debris, technology, and people from infinite dimensions.

Space here is recursive. If you walk away from the city center, you will eventually arrive back at it from the other side. There is no escape.

The City is ruled by The Head, a mysterious council that enforces their three laws through The Hand. They employ a force of terrifying enforcers known as Fingers to crush all who would break these sacred tenants.

Below them are the Circles—syndicates, cults, and corporations that run the day-to-day chaos of the Gutters and the Rises. You work for them, or you fight them.

The rules are simple: 1. Don't interfere with the Head, Heart, or Hand. 2. Pay your taxes on time. 3. Don't use dimensional magic.

docs.title: The System: Professional Violence
docs.text: Limbo splits the game into two distinct modes of play, recognizing that negotiating a hostage situation and fighting a psychic demon require different mechanical tools.

docs.title: 1. The Narrative Grid
docs.text:
Outside of combat, you solve problems using The Grid—a matrix of Attributes (Mind, Body, Spirit) and Approaches (Force, Flow, Resistance).

Power Flow Resistance BODY Brawn Reflex Grit MIND Intellect Wits Focus SOUL Presence Instinct Willpower

docs.title: 2. The Combat Loop
docs.text:
When violence starts, the game shifts to a rigid, tactical framework.

  • Pairwise Resolution: Every attack is a contested roll. If your Defense die beats their Attack die, you take no damage. If you win by double, you Break them.
  • Hardware vs. Software: Your Shell (Weapon) determines your potential; your Moves determine your technique.
  • High Lethality: HP is low. Damage is high.

docs.title: Who Are You?
docs.text:
You are not a hero. You're just someone trying to survive.

Whether you are a human scavenger or something stranger, you are defined by your ambition. You start as a grunt in the Gutters, fighting for Cred (XP) and Sal (Cash).

If you survive the politics of the Circles and the horrors of the Void, you might just become powerful enough to challenge the Head itself.

docs.title: Where to Start?
docs.text:
• Core Rules: Learn how the Dice Pool and Anomaly Tiers work.
  • The Classes: Choose your path.
  • The World: Explore the history of the Heart, the Hand, and the Inhabitants.

docs.title: Core Rules
docs.text:
Limbo is split into two states: The Hustle and The Fight. Both states use entirely different resolution mechanics.

Resolution Mechanics

Limbo RPG uses entirely different resolution mechanics for combat rolls and out-of-combat rolls. Your out-of-combat stats won't help you in combat, and vice versa.

docs.title: The Hustle
docs.text:
When you are exploring, socializing, or investigating, the game uses The Grid. This is a 3x3 grid of Attributes, Approaches, and their associated Skills.

  • Each Attribute and Approach pair creates a Skill.
  • Attributes and Approaches go from 0-5. Skills go from 0-10.
  • To make a check, you roll dice equal to your Skill. Any 4s, 5s, or 6s count as Beats. If you have more Beats than the Difficulty, you pass the Check.
  • If you have more 1s than 6s, you experience a Bane: an additional negative effect.
  • If you have more 6s than 1s, you experience a Boon: an additional positive effect.

docs.title: The Fight
docs.text:
When you engage in combat with one or more other entities, the game shifts. Unlike most other tabletop RPGs, Limbo uses a completely abstract positioning system -- tracking where you are on the battlefield is simply irrelevant. To make or survive attacks, you rely on the Clash system.

  • There are no passive armor classes. All attack rolls are contested rolls between the attacker and defender.
  • Combat is brutal. If you don't use all your tools effectively, you will die.
  • Eliminate the opposition while managing your Focus Points (EP) and Allegiance Actions.

docs.title: Anomaly Tiers
docs.text:
Limbo is not static. The deeper you go, or the longer you survive, the harder the universe pushes back.

  • Every threat, weapon, and entity in the game is assigned an Anomaly Tier (AT).
  • A Tier III weapon deals drastically more damage than a Tier I weapon. A Tier V horror operates on a level of physics that a Tier I scavenger cannot comprehend.
  • In Combat, damage scales linearly the difference in Tier between combatants.

Survival Tip

Combat can be a fail state. In many RPGs, combat is the best way to earn XP. In Limbo, combat is a resource drain that risks permanent death. Cred (XP) is earned by completing objectives, not by killing grunts. Fight only when you have to.

docs.title: Anomaly Tier Reference Table
docs.text:
Everything falls somewhere on this table, from speed to strength to social influence. It's used as a benchmark for many out-of-combat Moves and Features. Player characters also have an Anomaly Tier, though this caps at VII.

Tier Designation Scope (Size / Range) Social (Influence / Cost) Narrative Benchmark I Unknown Human-sized / Same location One person / Free Throwing a rock; Walking speed; Unarmored. II Acolyte Tall Creature / Touch Small Group / Pocket change A pistol shot; An Olympic athlete; Padded leather. III Vigilance Large Animal / Small Room Local Celebrity / Low cost A rifle shot; Fast animal; Kevlar vest. IV Warning Huge Animal / Large Room Gang Leader / Common purchase Lightning strike; Average vehicle; Heavy armor. V Oracle Small Building / Entire Building District Commander / Expensive Small explosion; Fast vehicle; Armored transport. VI Tyrant Large Building / City Block City Council / Unaffordable Large explosion; Airplane speed; Bank vault. VII Apex Hundreds of feet / District Large Circle / Circle Treasury Building collapse; Supersonic; Bunker. VIII Eclipse Miles / Several Districts The Hand / Head Treasury Nuke; Faster than sight; Invulnerable. IX City A Country / The City The Head / All Wealth Planet-buster; Instant travel; Functionally Invincible. X Heart Cosmic / Dimension A Law of Nature / Incalculable Black hole; Acausal movement; Universal truth.

docs.title: The Cycle of Play
docs.text: Time in Limbo RPG is tracked by Scenes and Chapters.

docs.title: Scenes
docs.text:
Scenes are individual vignettes. A Scene is a single encounter or challenge, and is often self-contained. Examples of Scenes include:

  • Talking to a shopkeeper.
  • Getting into a fight.
  • Bypassing a locked door.

Typically, if the GM establishes a new environment or situation, or begins or ends combat, that's a new Scene.

docs.title: Chapters
docs.text:
Chapters are collections of Scenes. Rather than being a single gameplay session, Chapters can be less than one, or multiple (though more often than not they're at least one full session, if not more). A Chapter is a self-contained arc that begins and ends at a "breakpoint" in the story. Examples of Chapters include:

  • Breaking someone out of prison.
  • Attending a party in the Rises.
  • Assassinating a target.

Typically, if the GM establishes a new primary objective, that's a new Chapter.

docs.title: Focus Points  (FP)
docs.text: Focus Points are used to power certain Utility Moves and to Push Checks. They regenerate fully at the start of a new Chapter, up to their limit (6).

docs.title: Skills, Approaches, and Attributes
docs.text:
All narrative checks are made by referencing the Grid, a 3x3 grid of Attributes (Body, Mind, and Soul) and Approaches (Power, Flow, Resistance). Each Attribute and Approach pair creates a Skill, whose value is the sum of its parents values.

Power Flow Resistance BODY Brawn Reflex Grit MIND Intellect Wits Focus SOUL Presence Instinct Willpower
  • Brawn is used when smashing doors down, intimidating someone with a show of force, or dragging something heavy. It's using physical might to get your way.
  • Reflex is used when jumping between buildings, navigating silently, pickpocketing a keycard. It's using dexterity to get your way.
  • Grit is used when surviving harsh environments, marching long treks, or withstanding the side effects of drugs. It's using your constitution to get your way.
  • Intellect is used when attempting to solve a cipher, navigating a maze, or jury-rigging a generator. It's using your raw smarts to get your way.
  • Wits is used when hacking into a local network, investigating a crime scene, or noticing details others might miss. It's using your mental flexibility to get your way.
  • Focus is used when sifting through pages of documents, ignoring distractions, or carefully performing a complex task. It's using your mental fortitude to get your way.
  • Presence is used when inspiring subordinates, terrifying someone, or performing a ballad. It's using your raw force of personality to get your way.
  • Instinct is used when empathizing with someone, seeing through lies, or interacting with animals. It's using empathy to get your way.
  • Willpower is used when ignoring a bribe, surviving a psychic attack, or avoiding someone's influence. It's using your emotional fortitude to get your way.

Each Attribute and Approach can go from 0 to 5, and each Skill is the sum of the Attribute and Approach, so they can go from 0 to 10.

docs.title: Making The Check
docs.text:
When making a check, choose which Skill you want to roll, and determine its value. Then, roll that many six-sided dice (d6). On a 4, 5, or 6, the die counts as a Beat. The number of Beats you score is then compared to the Difficulty of the check, which the GM determines. If the number of Beats meets or exceeds the Difficulty, you succeed at what you were trying to do. Otherwise, you fail.

If you roll more 6s than 1s, you get a Boon, meaning you get an additional positive effect regardless of whether you succeed or fail. Conversely, if you roll more 1s than 6s, you get a Bane, meaning you get an additional negative effect regardless of whether you succeed or fail.

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
The table below is a list of common Difficulties and their narrative descriptions. GMs should choose a Difficulty that best suits the situation. Keep in mind some checks may be impossible to succeed on.

Difficulty Value Description Common 1 Might take some effort for some people. Standard 2 Many might fail, many might succeed. Challenging 3 Uncertain for most people. Hard 4 Challenging even for the skilled. Extreme 5 Impossible for most people. Superhuman 6 Noticeably beyond the capacity for a normal human. Infeasible 7 Outstanding even among superhumans. Impossible 8+ Uncertain even for the strongest.

docs.title: Pushing Checks
docs.text: Checks can be Pushed to grant additional dice. When making a check, you may spend any number of FP to add that many additional dice to your check.

docs.title: Assisted Checks
docs.text: Other party members can also Assist on checks. Each person assisting must narratively justify how they help, and if they can, the player making the check adds dice equal to 1/2 of each assistant's Skill, rounded down (to a minimum of 1). A player can only be Assisted by up to 2 people at once.

docs.title: Resting
docs.text: Sometimes, you need to take a breather. When you choose to relax or sleep for at least 8 hours, you are considered Resting. When you Rest, the Scene automatically ends and you regain 12 HP (being Bloodied means you only regain 6, while being Mortal means you do not regain any). While Resting, and only while resting, you may swap weapons and rearrange your Repertoire.

docs.title: The Fight (Combat)
docs.text: Combat in Limbo uses an abstract, turn-based system. To make or survive attacks, you rely on The Clash.

docs.title: Allegiances and Turns
docs.text:
Combatants are divided into Allegiances—groups that share a singular goal (e.g., The Party vs. The Gang).

  • Turn Order: The GM determines the order in which Allegiances act. If there is no clear priority, the players’ Allegiance goes first.
  • The Round: When an Allegiance starts its turn, all members of that Allegiance may act. They may resolve their actions in any order they choose. Once an Allegiance has finished, no member may act again until the next round.

docs.title: The Action Economy
docs.text: While the Allegiance shares a "Turn," individuals are limited by their Action Slots.

docs.title: Action Slots
docs.text:
Your Anomaly Tier determines how many times you can act in a single round.

Anomaly Tier Action Slots I - II 1 III - V 2 VI - VIII 3 IX - X 4
  • Spending Slots: You may declare one Offensive Move or Action per Slot.
  • Defense is Free: Using a Defensive Move is reactive and does not cost an Action Slot.

docs.title: The Repertoire
docs.text:
You may only bring a limited amount of mental and physical muscle memory into a fight.

  • Limit: You may have a maximum of 8 Moves prepared in your Repertoire at any time.
  • Scope: This includes Offensive Moves, Defensive Moves, and Passives (including those from Genetics or Cybernetics).
  • Swapping: You may reconfigure your Repertoire during a Rest or by visiting a Doctor (for Genetics and Cybernetics).

docs.title: Energy Points (EP)
docs.text:
Moves require energy and concentration.

  • Starting EP: Everyone begins combat with 3 EP.
  • Cap: You can hold a maximum of 6 EP.
  • Regeneration: At the start of your Allegiance's turn, you regenerate 1 EP.
  • Cost: If you have 0 EP, you cannot use Moves that require an EP cost ( EP > 0). You must use Moves with no EP cost or wait to rebuild your pool.

docs.title: The Clash
docs.text:
All combat is resolved through The Clash.

  1. Declaration: The attacker picks a valid Offensive Move and a target.
  2. Response: The target picks a valid Defensive Move.
    • Note: Armor is passive and does not count as a Move. You must select a Defensive Move from your Weapon, Class, or Subclass in order to defend.
  3. Roll: Both parties roll six-sided dice (d6) equal to their Move's Dice stat, and add their Move's Power stat to each die rolled.
  4. Sort: Arrange all dice on each side from highest to lowest to form Pairs.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Compare the dice Pairwise—highest with highest, 2nd highest with 2nd highest, and so on.

  • Win: If your die is higher than or equal to the opponent's die, you Win the Pair.
    • Effect: Add an instance of the Move's Damage to your total.
  • Loss: If your die is lower than the opponent's die, you suffer a Loss.
    • Effect: You deal no damage. You likely suffer Harm from the opponent's Win.
  • Unopposed: If a die has no counterpart on the opponent's side (one pool is larger than the other), it is an automatic Win.
    • Effect: Add an instance of the Move's Damage to your total.
  • Break: If a die’s modified value is twice that of the opponent's die (e.g., 8 vs 4), it is a Break.
    • Effect: All subsequent dice on the opponent's side are removed from the Clash immediately. The Break itself counts as a standard Win.

docs.title: Harm Calculation
docs.text:
HP loss is referred to as Harm. It is calculated after all dice in a Clash are resolved.

  1. Sum Damage: Sum all Damage from Wins (Opposed and Unopposed).
  2. Apply Scaling: Determine the Tier Disparity between the Attacker (Weapon/Anomaly Tier) and the Target (Armor Tier). Add or subtract the Bonus/Penalty Damage from the Scaling Table to your sum. This is your Gross Harm.
    • Note: If you score 0 Wins, you deal 0 Harm. Scaling bonuses never apply to a failed Clash.
  3. Subtract AV: Subtract the target's total Armor Value (AV) (Base AV + Armor Quality) from the Gross Harm.
  4. Apply Harm: Apply the remaining amount to the target's HP. This is the Net Harm.
    • Note: True Harm cannot be mitigated or avoided.

Harm Versus Damage

Harm is a number subtracted from a target's HP. Damage is a stat on Moves used to calculate Harm. If something says "increase Damage," that means increase the DMG stat.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Example 1: Wins and Scaling (Peer)
  • Roland (Tier III) attacks with a Class Move: 4 / 1 / 6
  • Red Guard (Tier III) defends with a Defensive Move: 3 / 0 / 0
  • The Stats: Roland is Tier III. The Guard is Tier III (Disparity 0). The Guard is wearing Medium Armor (Base 2 AV).
  • The Roll:
    • Roland: 5, 3, 1 (Add +1 PWR) 6, 4, 2
    • Guard: 4, 4, 3 (Add +0 PWR) 4, 4, 3
  • The Comparison:
    1. 6 vs 4: Roland Win. (+6 Dmg).
    2. 4 vs 4: Roland Win (Ties go to Attacker). (+6 Dmg).
    3. 2 vs 3: Guard Win. (Prevents Damage).
  • The Result:
    • Roland: (12 Harm from Wins) + 0 Scaling (Tier III vs III) = 12 Gross Harm. 12 - 2 AV = 10 Net Harm to Guard.
    • Guard: 1 Win. Prevents 1 instance of damage. 0 Net Harm to Roland.
Example 2: Breaks and Tier Disparity
  • Amon (Tier IV Weapon / Armor) attacks with a Heavy Weapon: 2 / 4 / 8
  • Thug (Tier II Weapon / Armor) attacks with a Swarm Class Move: 5 / 0 / 2
  • The Stats: Amon has a Tier IV Weapon and the Thug has Tier II Armor (Disparity 2, or +12 Harm to the thug). Amon targets Light Armor (Base 1 AV). Thug targets Heavy Armor (Base 3 AV).
  • The Roll:
    • Amon: 5, 3 (Add +4 PWR) 9, 7
    • Thug: 4, 3, 3, 2, 1 (Add +0 PWR) 4, 3, 3, 2, 1
  • The Comparison:
    1. 9 vs 4: Break (9 > 2x4). Amon Win.
      • Effect: All subsequent Thug dice (3, 3, 2, 1) are removed.
    2. 7 vs N/A: Unopposed. Amon Win.
  • The Result:
    • Amon: (16 Harm from Wins) + 0 Scaling = 16 Gross. 16 - 1 AV = 15 Net Harm to Thug.
    • Thug: 0 Wins. 0 Net Harm to Amon.

docs.title: Phases of Combat
docs.text: Combat is split into distinct phases to help adjudicate rules. There are two distinct cycles: the macro cycle (the Allegiance turn) and the micro cycle (the Clash sequence).

docs.title: Phase 1: The Upkeep Phase
docs.text:
• All members of the Allegiance gain 1 EP.
  • Then, all "Start of Turn" effects trigger. If there are conflicting triggers, the active Allegiance decides the order.

docs.title: Phase 2: The Action Phase
docs.text:
• The free-flow main phase. Any members of the Allegiance can take Actions in any order, including interweaving them between members (character A acts, character B acts, then character A acts again).
  • This is where EP and Action Slots are spent, Moves are activated, and Actions taken.

docs.title: Phase 3: The Cleanup Phase
docs.text:
• All "End of Turn" effects (such as Bleed's Harm) trigger. If there are conflicting triggers, the active Allegiance decides the order.
  • All statuses decay by 3.
  • Once this Phase ends, initiative is passed to the next Allegiance.

docs.title: Step 1: Targeting Step
docs.text:
The following occur in order:

  • 1.1 Declaration: the attacker declares an Offensive Move, pays the EP cost, then declares a target. If the EP cost cannot be paid, the Move cannot be activated. When declaring an Offensive Move that is not Innate, you must choose which of your equipped Weapons you are using to make that Move. Only the effects of that Weapon's Feature are applied.
  • 1.2 Intervention: this is the short window where the target may be changed. The target may be changed multiple times.
  • 1.3 Lock-In: the final target is locked in.
  • 1.4 Defense: the final target declares a Defensive Move and pays the EP cost. If the EP cost cannot be paid, the Move cannot be activated. If no Defensive Moves can be activated, the defender rolls 0 dice and all the attacker's dice are considered Unopposed.

docs.title: Step 2: Rolling Step
docs.text:
The following occur in order:

  • 2.1 Rolling: all dice ( DIE) are rolled simultaneously.
  • 2.2 Manipulation: all dice manipulation abilities happen (re-rolls, changing values, etc.)
  • 2.3 Power Addition: Power ( PWR) is added to each.
  • 2.4 Arrangement: All dice on each side are arranged from highest to lowest and paired off.
  • 2.5 Calculation: Pairs are evaluated one by one from highest to lowest. If a Break occurs, all subsequent dice on the opposing side are removed before they can be evaluated. Each Win adds an instance of the Move's Harm to its target's Harm Pool.

docs.title: Step 3: Application Step
docs.text:
The following occur in order:

  • 3.1 Gross Harm Modification: The Harm Pool (Gross Harm) is modified by AV, Anomaly Tier scaling (if Gross Harm from Wins is greater than 0), and any reductions/additions. Multipliers are added from highest to lowest, with positive multipliers being applied first.
  • 3.2 Net Harm Application: The finalized Harm Pool (Net Harm) is subtracted from each target's HP.
  • 3.3 Keyword and Status Application: All status effects inflicted by the Move are applied. All "On Win," "On Break," and "On Loss" triggers are applied.

docs.title: Statuses
docs.text:
Status Effects in Limbo stack, and usually have two effects: a triggered effect, which typically deals True Harm equal to stacks, and a passive effect that doesn't scale and applies for as long as an entity has stacks.

For example, Tremor deals True Harm equal to stacks when the afflicted target is Broken, and while the target has any stacks of Tremor, they get a non-scaling -2 AV penalty.

docs.title: Status Decay
docs.text: Statuses decay by 3 stacks at the end of the afflicted target's Allegiance turn, to a minimum of 0.

docs.title: Conditions
docs.text: Conditions are binary states. You either have one or you don't. They're usually only dispelled by certain circumstances, such as healing above a threshold (Bloodied, Mortal) or attacking/getting hit (Hidden).

docs.title: The Economy
docs.text: The economy in Limbo is split into two main resources: Cred (XP) and Sal (Money).

docs.title: Cred
docs.text: Cred is the main resource you spend to improve your character. For a detailed breakdown on it, reference the Leveling section. In short, it's split into two types: Fight Cred and Hustle Cred.

docs.title: Fight Cred
docs.text: Fight Cred is spent on purchasing or leveling up Combat Moves (Offensive Moves, Defensive Moves, and Passive Moves). Each Level, you gain 1 Fight Cred.

docs.title: Hustle Cred
docs.text: Hustle Cred is spent in one of two ways: increasing an Attribute or Approach, or purchasing or leveling up a Utility Move. Each Level, you gain 1 Hustle Cred.

docs.title: Sal
docs.text: Sal is the main resource used to purchase in-game goods such as armor, weapons, food, or bribes. It's a copper coin backed by salt distributed by the Head.

docs.title: Common Sal Costs
docs.text: Item Cost A day's worth of cheap food 150 Sal A kilogram of real meat 3,000 Sal A Rest at a cheap hostel 300 Sal A gram of pure salt 10 Sal A pint of potato beer 30 Sal A bottle of hard liquor 150 Sal A Cycle's rent in the Gutters 7,500 Sal A Cycle's rent in the lower Rises 90,000 Sal A Cycle's rent in the upper Rises 450,00 Sal A pack of mushroom cigarettes 80 Sal ### Weapon Sal Costs Tier Cost I 400 Sal II 2,500 Sal III 8,000 Sal IV 30,000 Sal V 150,000 Sal VI 500,000 Sal VII 2.5 million Sal VIII 8 million Sal IX 30 million Sal X Unaffordable ### Medical Sal Costs Item Cost Emergency Surgery (heal while Mortal) 250 Sal/HP

docs.title: Scaling and Quality
docs.text: This section defines the math used to calculate your Gross Harm and Armor Value.

docs.title: The Scaling Table
docs.text:
Use this table to determine the flat bonus added to your Harm based on your Anomaly Tier (AT) (for Class Moves) or your Weapon Tier (for Weapon Moves) versus your target's Armor Tier. Note that Harm cannot be reduced below 0.

Anomaly Tier Disparity Bonus/Penalty Harm +0 +0 +1/-1 +4/-4 +2/-2 +8/-8 +3/-3 +12/-12 +4/-4 +16/-16 +5/-5 +20/-20 +6/-6 +24/-24

The Zero-Harm Rule

Scaling bonuses are only applied if you deal at least 1 Harm in the Clash. If your Move does 0 Harm, Scaling doesn't apply to it. If you lose every Pair, the Move does 0 Harm, meaning Scaling doesn't apply to it.

docs.title: Weapons, Armor, and Mods
docs.text:
Weapons and Armor can be modified during a Rest with Mods. Mods can be purchased from vendors or stripped from existing Weapons and Armor, then applied to your own.

Repertoire Capacity

Your Repertoire can only have 8 Moves in it, including Moves from all equipped weapons.

docs.title: Equipping Weapons
docs.text:
Weapons are either 1 or 2-handed. You may only have a number of Weapons equipped with a total "handed" value equal to your character's number of hands. For example, if your character has 3 hands, they can either equip 3 1-handed Weapons or a 1-handed Weapon and a 2-handed Weapon. You may only change what weapons you have equipped during a Rest, when you can reconfigure your Repertoire.

Weapon Features

You have all the Features from all Equipped Weapons.

docs.title: Mod Capacity
docs.text:
Weapons have a Mod Capacity, which is how many Mods they can have equipped at a time. This depends on the Weapon's Anomaly Tier.

Tier Capacity I 0 II 1 III-IV 2 V-VI 3 VII+ 4 ### Modifying Moves There are two types of Mods: Global Mods, which modify all Moves on a Weapon, and Local Mods, which modify a single Move on the Weapon. A Move can only have 1 Local Mod targeting it at a time.

Move Targeting

Mods can only target Moves from the Weapon they're attached to. If you have two of the same Weapon, you must pick which Weapon each of your Moves come from. Mods apply to individual Weapons, meaning you can build two of the same weapon differently on the same character.

docs.title: Armor Capacity
docs.text:
Armor has a Mod Capacity, just like Weapons, but Armor's Capacity scales off of the weight of the Armor rather than its Anomaly Tier. As a rule, the heavier the Armor, the more AV it provides but the fewer Mods it can fit.

Armor Weight Capacity Light 3 Medium 2 Heavy 1 ## Adding and Removing Mods Mods can be added or removed during a Rest. Adding a Mod can be done freely. Removing a Mod requires a Difficulty 3 Intellect or Wits check, taking it to a vendor (200 Sal), or purchasing a Jury-Rig Kit (250 Sal). Removed Mods go into your inventory.

If you choose to make the check to remove a Mod, the following results may happen: - Success: you remove the Mod. It goes into your inventory. - Failure: you fail to remove the Mod. - Boon: Regain 1 FP as you gain some insight into how the Mod works. - Bane: You break the Mod. It is nonfunctional until you take it to a vendor (200 Sal) or use a Jury-Rig Kit. Broken Mods cannot be attached to Weapons or Armor.

docs.title: Move Anatomy
docs.text: In the City of Limbo, every ability, biological mutation, and tactical maneuver is represented by a Move Card. While all cards share a unified design, their colors, icons, and Type Badges identify their role in the game and how they interact with your Repertoire.

docs.title: Identifying a Move
docs.text:
A Move Card is defined by several key components that dictate its cost and effect:

  • Type Badge & Color: Located at the top-left, the badge (e.g., OFF, UTL) and the card's border color indicate its category.
  • The Cost:
    • EP (Energy Points): Used for Fight Moves. You regenerate 1 EP at the start of your turn and can hold a maximum of 6.
    • FP (Focus Points): Used for Hustle Moves. These regenerate at the start of a new Chapter and can hold a maximum of 6.
  • Stats: Combat moves display Dice (), Power (), and Damage ().
  • Keywords: Bolded terms like Flow, Vicious, or Innate that trigger specific rules.

docs.title: Fight Moves (The Repertoire)
docs.text: Fight Moves represent your combat muscle memory. You may only have 8 Fight Moves equipped in your Repertoire at any time, and they can only be swapped during a Rest.

docs.title: Offensive Moves
docs.text: Offensive Moves are used during your Allegiance’s Action Phase. Spending an Action Slot allows you to declare one Offensive Move to initiate a Clash against a target.

docs.title: OFF Example Offense
docs.text:
DIE: X // PWR: X // DMG: X // EP: X

Rules text for an attack.

docs.title: Defensive Moves
docs.text: Defensive Moves are used reactively when you are the target of a Clash. Using a Defensive Move is free and does not cost an Action Slot. If you have no Defensive Moves in your Repertoire, you roll 0 dice.

docs.title: DEF Example Defense
docs.text:
DIE: X // PWR: X // DMG: X // EP: X

Rules text for a defense.

docs.title: Passive Moves
docs.text: Passive Moves provide constant combat benefits. Unlike Offensive or Defensive moves, they do not require a Clash to function, but they do occupy one of your 8 Repertoire slots.

docs.title: PAS Example Passive
docs.text: Ongoing combat rules text.

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Features provide constant combat benefits, similar to Passive Moves. Unlike Passive Moves, however, Features do not take up a Repertoire Slot. They often add mechanics or new systems to the game.

docs.title: FEA Example Feature
docs.text: New mechanic/system/rules text.

docs.title: Hustle Moves (The Toolkit)
docs.text: Hustle Moves represent your innate nature and specialized training. You can possess an infinite number of Hustle Moves; they never count against your Repertoire limit.

docs.title: Utility Moves
docs.text: Utility Moves are active abilities used during narrative play. They typically require the expenditure of Focus Points (FP) to achieve extraordinary results.

docs.title: UTL Example Utility
docs.text:
Cost: X FP

Narrative effect text.

docs.title: Trait Moves
docs.text: Trait Moves represent passive narrative qualities or expertise. They often grant Automatic Beats or Boons on specific Grid checks (such as Brawn or Wits).

docs.title: TRT Example Trait
docs.text: Constant narrative benefit.

docs.title: Character Creation
docs.text:
Before you can survive the City, you must first become a part of it.

In Limbo, you're defined not by who you are but what you can do. This page serves as a basic guide for character creation.

docs.title: 1. Your Background
docs.text: First, you must determine your character's history. This is done through the Background system, which walks you through your life, relationships, and goals.

docs.title: 2. Your Stats
docs.text: Next, you must determine your stats; namely, your Attributes, Approaches, and Skills. Assign your standard array (0, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3) to determine your proficiency in the nine Skills used during narrative play.

docs.title: 3. Your Class
docs.text: Determining your Class is the next step in character creation. You may choose one of the Classes and invest your starting Cred (2 Fight Cred and 2 Hustle Cred) into Moves.

docs.title: 4. Your Equipment
docs.text: Finally, you must determine what equipment, biological mutations, and cybernetic augmentations your character has.

docs.title: Placeholder HP Heading
docs.text: Player Characters have 100 HP.

docs.title: Leveling and Progression
docs.text: In the City of Limbo, power is not just found; it is built through the accumulation of Cred. As you survive encounters and complete objectives for the various Circles of the City, you earn the resources necessary to refine your combat repertoire and enhance your narrative capabilities.

docs.title: Earning Cred
docs.text:
Progression is split into two distinct tracks. Each time you level up (to a maximum of Level 20), you gain:

  • 1 Fight Cred: Used to purchase or level up Combat Moves (Offensive, Defensive, and Passive).
  • 1 Hustle Cred: Used to increase Attributes/Approaches or purchase/level up Utility Moves.

You begin your career at Level 1 with 2 Fight Cred and 2 Hustle Cred.

docs.title: Fight Progression
docs.text: Fight Cred is your primary resource for combat specialization. You may spend it in the following ways:

docs.title: 1. Unlocking Moves
docs.text:
• New Move: Spend 1 Fight Cred to unlock any Move from your Class. That Move starts at Rank 0.
  • Subclass Entry: Purchasing your first Move from a Subclass tree permanently locks you into that specialization and prevents you from purchasing Moves from other Subclasses.

docs.title: 2. Ranking Up Moves
docs.text:
Each Move can be Ranked up 3 times. Each Rank costs 1 Fight Cred.

  • When Ranking up a Move, you choose one of two available Upgrades (Rank 1, 2, or 3) provided in your Class dossier.
  • Once an upgrade is chosen, its effects are permanently added to that Move's card.

docs.title: Hustle Progression
docs.text:
Hustle Cred allows you to broaden your influence and survivability within the City.

  • The Grid: Spend 1 Hustle Cred to increase any Attribute (Body, Mind, Soul) or Approach (Power, Flow, Resistance) by 1, up to a maximum of 5.
  • Utility & Traits: Spend 1 Hustle Cred to unlock a new Utility Move or Trait from your Class list or the Universal list.

docs.title: Repertoire Management
docs.text:
While you can unlock and level many Moves, your Repertoire—the Moves you can actually use in a fight—is limited to 8 slots.

  • Only Fight Moves (Offensive, Defensive, Passive) occupy these slots.
  • You may only swap which Moves are in your Repertoire or rearrange your upgrades during a Rest.
  • You may only change which Weapons you're wielding during a Rest. If you choose to wield fewer Weapon Shells than your Limbs can allow, all remaining Weapon Shells become Unarmed.

docs.title: Core Classes
docs.text:
Select a class to view its mechanical identity, core dossiers, and specialized subclasses.

[Operator] — The Weaponry Specialist

Hone your skills with weaponry to maintain pressure in a fight.

  • [Chain]: Overwhelm foes with a relentless barrage of repeated attacks.
  • [Verdict]: Focus your resolve to strike once and strike true.
[Nitro] — The High-Stakes Bruiser

Build overwhelming power by sacrificing your own safety and HP.

  • [Inferno]: Burn your own vitality to grant yourself powerful combat buffs.
  • [Martyr]: Sacrifice your HP to provide vital support and buffs to your party.
[Lux] — The Hard-Light Architect

Manifest and command physical constructs of solid light.

  • [Aegis]: Deploy hard-light barriers to protect your allies from harm.
  • [Sword]: Summon crystalline weaponry to strike from unexpected angles.
[Graft] — The Body Alterer

Rewrite biological limits by altering your own form or the bodies of others.

  • [Marrow]: Distort the biology of your targets, for better or for worse.
  • [Chimera]: Mutate your own body to adapt to any combat situation.
[Aether] — The Elemental Weaver

Channel and control the raw elements of the multiverse.

  • [Cycle]: Specialize in a single element to master its destructive potential.
  • [Maelstrom]: Unleash unpredictable and volatile wild magic effects.
[Pattern] — The Info-Broker

Exercise absolute control over information and neural pathways.

  • [Architect]: Analyze systems and entities to find their fundamental flaws.
  • [Synapse]: Invade and manipulate the minds of your enemies.
[Mantra] — The Willpower Weapon

Weaponize your own internal resolve and manipulate the threads of fate.

  • [Banner]: Lead from the front, inspiring allies with your sheer presence.
  • [Entropy]: Distort the laws of probability to manipulate luck and odds.
[Voice] — The Commanding Presence

Use social influence and commands as your primary weapon of war.

  • [Herald]: Coordinate your party with tactical buffs and battlefield commands.
  • [Crown]: Debilitate foes through the sheer weight of your personality.
[Motion] — The Kinetic Master

Manipulate the fundamental laws of physics to control movement.

  • [Vector]: Alter the speed and direction of physical objects and entities.
  • [Grav]: Take absolute command over local gravity wells.
[Fabric] — The Physical Shaper

Control and reshape the physical world and its chemical properties.

  • [Catalyst]: Brew potent potions and lethal toxins in the heat of battle.
  • [Tinker]: Rapidly create and deploy mechanical devices and traps.
[Engine] — The Construct Controller

Create, hijack, and enhance artificial technology and constructs.

  • [Forge]: Buff allies' gear and enhance the effectiveness of their Moves.
  • [Override]: Hijack and turn enemy technology against its owners.
[Chronos] — The Time Weaver

Command the flow of time itself to gain a tactical edge.

  • [Oracle]: Peer into the immediate future to anticipate enemy actions.
  • [Moment]: Accelerate or decelerate your own personal time stream.
[Legion] — The Summoner

Command summoned entities and biological thralls.

  • [Crucible]: Create and weaponize a unique, personal familiar.
  • [Anima]: Bend the animals of Limbo and the Void to your will.
[Heart] — The Unyielding Wall

Endure incredible hardship and turn your own pain into power.

  • [Bastion]: Sacrifice your own safety to mitigate damage for your allies.
  • [Rage]: Grow in physical strength as your allies suffer harm.
[Wraith] — The Phantom Stalker

Utilize superior stealth and blinding speed to hunt your prey.

  • [Hollow]: Vanish from sight and erase your existence from the senses of others.
  • [Mercury]: Strike and move at speeds that defy the human eye.
[Null] — The Void Channeler

Channel the absolute emptiness of the Void to erase existence.

  • [Stillness]: Neutralize Artifice and silence the world around you.
  • [Eraser]: Permanently remove your targets from the physical plane.

docs.title: Operator
docs.text:
A knife is the most powerful form of Artifice, second only to a gun.

An Operator excels at weapon use, forgoing fancy powers for strict adherence to tactical domination. Build Momentum with successful hits, and cash it in for big rewards.

docs.title: FEA Momentum
docs.text:
Whenever you score at least 1 Win in a Clash or score a Break, gain 1 Momentum (max 6). Momentum doesn't decay naturally during combat, but it clears at the end of combat.

Setup X: Moves with this Keyword allow you to spend X Momentum on them to activate the associated effect when declaring the Move. If a Move has multiple Setup costs, you must activate them separately.

docs.title: Repertoire
docs.text: Manage your 8-Move Repertoire limit carefully between Rests.

docs.title: OFF Neutralize
docs.text:
DIE: 4 // PWR: 1 // DMG: 4 // EP: 2

Setup 2: Each Win reduces the target's DIE by 1 on their next Clash, to a minimum of 1. If DIE cannot be reduced any more, reduce PWR by 1 instead.

Rank 1

  • Increase DMG by 1.
  • Decrease EP by 1.

Rank 2

  • Increase PWR by 1.
  • Gain Area 1.

Rank 3

  • On 2 Wins, next Win is an automatic Break.
  • On Break: Reduce the target's DMG by half on their next Move. If the Move would deal 0 Harm, gain 1 Momentum.

docs.title: OFF Shock and Awe
docs.text:
DIE: 5 // PWR: 3 // DMG: 3 // EP: 3

Setup X: This Move gains Afflict Tremor X.

Rank 1

  • Add Vicious.
  • Increase DMG by 1.

Rank 2

  • Increase X by 1.
  • Decrease EP by 1.

Rank 3

  • Add Area 1, but decrease DMG by 1.
  • If the target's DIE is reduced for this Clash, this Move gains Area 1

docs.title: DEF Weave Through
docs.text:
DIE: 4 // PWR: 1 // DMG: 0 // EP: 1

On Win: reduce the next instance of Harm taken by 1.

Innate

Rank 1

  • Add Deflect
  • Add Reinforced

Rank 2

  • Increase PWR by 1.
  • Reduce the next instance of Harm taken by an additional 1.

Rank 3

  • Add Setup 2: this Move gains "On Win: all subsequent dice gain +1 PWR."
  • Add Setup 2: this Move gains "On Loss: Reduce the PWR of the opponent's next die by 1."

docs.title: PAS Cold Start
docs.text:
You begin each Fight Scene with 2 Momentum. You gain access to the Refund Action.

Refund: this Action does not cost an Action Slot, and can be used once per turn immediately after using a Move. Expend X Momentum and gain X EP, where X is the Move's EP.

Rank 1

  • Begin each fight scene with 3 Momentum instead.
  • Increase your maximum Momentum to 8.

Rank 2

  • You may use Refund +1 time per turn.
  • You may use Refund on allies' Moves to grant them EP instead of you.

Rank 3

  • Gain X+1 EP instead if X is greater than 0.
  • This Action now costs an Action Slot. Gain twice X EP instead.

docs.title: UTL Well-Connected
docs.text:
Cost: 2 FP

You have contacts that can get items for you. Once per Chapter, you may use this Move to purchase an item less than 2,000 Sal that's illegal or regulated in your current area. It has a 25% markup if purchased this way, though this markup doesn't apply to the cost limit. This Move is unusable in the Depths.

docs.title: UTL Field Strip
docs.text:
Cost: 3 FP

You may use this Move to add or remove a Mod from a Weapon without a Check or Jury-Rig Kit.

docs.title: TRT Concealed Carry
docs.text: You may perfectly hide a one-Handed weapon of an Anomaly Tier half your own (min I) on your person. It is undetectable by standard visual inspection or pat-down.

docs.title: TRT Conditioned
docs.text: Gain two automatic Beats on all narrative Checks to withstand torture, drugs, exhaustion, or environmental effects.

docs.title: Subclasses
docs.text: Unlocking your first move in a subclass locks you into that path.

docs.title: Chain
docs.text:
A barrage of gunfire erupted from the stranger's coat. When the smoke cleared, all that was left of Wallace was a mass of flesh.

Chains excel in volume of fire and suppression, devastating targets with high Dice Moves and pressuring them with status application.

docs.title: FEA Blitz
docs.text: Upon scoring at least 1 Win in a Clash, you may expend any amount of Momentum to use a single Move without spending an Action Slot immediately after the current one resolves. The Move's maximum EP cannot exceed half the Momentum spent (rounded down). You still pay the EP.

docs.title: OFF Barrage
docs.text:
DIE: 6 // PWR: 0 // DMG: 2 // EP: 1

Afflict Tremor 1.

Setup 1: On 4+ Wins, apply 6 Tremor.

Rank 1

  • Apply Tremor on 3+ Wins instead.
  • Increase DMG by 1.

Rank 2

  • If the target has Tremor, increase PWR by 1.
  • If the target has Tremor, increase DIE by 1.

Rank 3

  • On Break: apply 12 Tremor instead.
  • Unopposed Dice apply twice as much Tremor.

docs.title: OFF Overwhelm
docs.text:
DIE: 5 // PWR: 0 // DMG: 3 // EP: 0

Setup 2: This Move gains "On Win: deal 1 True Harm to the target."

Rank 1

  • Increase DIE by 1.
  • If you used Blitz to activate this Move, it gains +2 DIE.

Rank 2

  • For each True Harm dealt to the target, apply 1 Shock.
  • If the target has a status effect and this Move scored at least 1 Win, they take an additional 3 True Harm after this Move resolves.

Rank 3

  • Deal 2 True Harm instead.
  • This Move gains Setup X: increase DIE by 1/2 X.

docs.title: DEF Counterstrike
docs.text:
DIE: 4 // PWR: 2 // DMG: 2 // EP: 2

When rolling dice for this Move, each natural 6 rolled adds +1 DIE to the pool. Roll these dice all at once, and they cannot generate additional dice.

Rank 1

  • Decrease EP by 1.
  • Increase DIE by 1.

Rank 2

  • Natural 6s rolled are immune to Breaks.
  • If this Move deals at least 6 Harm, add an additional 4 Harm to its Gross Harm Pool.

Rank 3

  • Add additional dice on 5s and 6s instead of just 6s.
  • The extra dice rolled can each add 1 additional die if they roll a natural 6.

docs.title: PAS Adrenaline
docs.text:
Gain an additional Action Slot this turn if you activated Blitz last turn and spent at least 6 Momentum on it.

Rank 1

  • Any Actions taken using this additional Action Slot cost -1 EP, to a minimum of 0.
  • If this Action Slot is used on an Action rather than a Move, refund its EP.

Rank 2

  • Also gain 1 EP.
  • Also gain 1 Momentum.

Rank 3

  • You only have to spend 3 Momentum on Blitz to trigger this Move.
  • Any Actions taken using this additional Action Slot gain +1 if they have at least 4 .

docs.title: UTL Breach and Clear
docs.text:
Cost: 2 FP

Activate this Move to bypass a locked door, window, or physical barrier violently. If this initiates a Fight Scene, you start with an additional Momentum. The GM may rule that a barrier is too hard to bypass, such as a blast door or brick wall.

docs.title: TRT Well-Trained
docs.text: Choose one Weapon Shell. Gain an automatic Boon on any narrative checks that involve brandishing, maintaining, or performing tricks with that Weapon Shell.

docs.title: Verdict
docs.text:
"Strike sure," he said, "or don't strike at all."

A Verdict Operator is all about precision and Power, overwhelming foes and benefitting from Breaks.

docs.title: FEA Breaker
docs.text: Whenever you score a Break, reduce the target's AV by your Momentum on your next Clash against them.

docs.title: OFF Shatter
docs.text:
DIE: 2 // PWR: 3 // DMG: 10 // EP: 2

Setup 1: increase PWR by 1.

Rank 1

  • Add "On Win: the target loses 1 AV for the next Clash"
  • Increase PWR by 1.

Rank 2

  • Decrease EP by 1.
  • Add Vicious.

Rank 3

  • Add "All Unopposed dice have double DMG"
  • On Break: the target's Armor is treated as 1 Anomaly Tier lower for the purposes of Scaling in this Clash.

docs.title: OFF Exploit
docs.text:
DIE: 1 // PWR: 4 // DMG: 18 // EP: 1

Setup 2: Add "On Break: reduce the target's next Move's PWR by 2."

Rank 1

  • Add Add Bulwark 3.
  • Decrease Setup cost to 1.

Rank 2

  • Decrease target's PWR by 3 instead.
  • Increase by 1, but decrease DMG by 8.

Rank 3

  • Change "On Break" to "On Win."
  • Decrease PWR by 3 instead.

docs.title: DEF Parry
docs.text:
DIE: 1 // PWR: 4 // DMG: 8 // EP: 1

This Move causes a Break if a die is 4+ higher than the opposing die instead of double.

Rank 1

  • Add "On Break: gain 2 EP."
  • Add Flow 1.

Rank 2

  • Cause a Break if a die is 3+ higher instead.
  • Increase :fontawesome-burst: DMG by 2.

Rank 3

  • Decrease EP to 0.
  • Add "On Win: Negate the next X dice in the Clash, where X is your Momentum."

docs.title: PAS One Shot
docs.text:
If you used only one Class or Subclass Move on your last Allegiance turn, increase the PWR of your next Weapon Move by 1.

Rank 1

  • This effect also applies if you haven't used a Class or Subclass Move this Allegiance turn.
  • Also add Vicious to the Weapon Move.

Rank 2

  • You may increase PWR by 3 instead if the Weapon Move is the first Action you take on your Allegiance turn. If you do, you may not use any Class or Subclass Moves this turn.
  • If that Weapon Move causes a Break, deal 3 True Harm to the target.

Rank 3

  • If that Weapon Move causes a Break, gain 1 additional Momentum.
  • Also increase the DMG of that Move by 50%.

docs.title: UTL Heavy Presence
docs.text:
Cost: 1 FP

When making a check that involves inspiring someone, coercing someone, or keeping your cool, you may activate this Move to gain automatic Beats equal to 1/2 your Anomaly Tier, to a minimum of 1.

docs.title: TRT Threat Detection
docs.text:
If you spend one minute examining a person, you may ask the GM one of the following questions:

  • Are they armed, even if it's concealed?
  • What's their Anomaly Tier?
  • What is their Armor Tier?

docs.title: Nitro
docs.text:
He burned so bright, but burnt out so quickly.

A Nitro excels at using their own body for fuel, sacrificing HP for potent effects. Generate Heat upon taking Harm to cash in for rewards.

docs.title: FEA Reactor
docs.text:
Torch X: Suffer X True Harm to activate a Move’s Torch effect. If a Move has multiple Torch costs, you must activate them individually. On Innate Moves, you may Torch 5 to increase a Move’s DMG by 50%, rounded down.

Heat Stacks: Whenever you take Harm from any source, gain 1 Heat (max 6). Spend 3 Heat to reduce the EP cost of your next Move by 1, to a minimum of 0.

docs.title: Repertoire
docs.text: Manage your 8-Move Repertoire limit carefully between Rests.

docs.title: OFF Ignition
docs.text:
DIE: 3 // PWR: 2 // DMG: 4 // EP: 1

Afflict Burn 3.

Innate.

Rank 1

  • Add "Torch 3: Afflict +1 Burn."
  • Add Flow 1.

Rank 2

  • Add Seeking.
  • Increase DMG by 1.

Rank 3

  • If you have at least 4 Heat, this Move gains +1 PWR.
  • If you have at least 4 Heat, this Move gains +1 and -1 DMG.

docs.title: OFF Soul Burn
docs.text:
DIE: 4 // PWR: 1 // DMG: 2 // EP: 2

Torch 4: This Move gains "On Win: heal 4 HP."

Innate.

Rank 1

  • Add Breach.
  • Add Torch 4: Decrease this Move's EP cost by 1.

Rank 2

  • Increase :matieral-dice-6: by 1 but increase Torch costs by 1.
  • Add "On Break: heal 6 HP."

Rank 3

  • Add Reinforced.
  • Increase PWR by X, where X is one third of your Heat.

docs.title: DEF Iron Will
docs.text:
DIE: 2 // PWR: 3 // DMG: 0 // EP: 0

Innate.

Bulwark 2

Rank 1

  • Add "On Use: gain 1 Heat."
  • Add "On Loss: gain 1 Heat."

Rank 2

  • Increase by 1.
  • Increase to Bulwark 3.

Rank 3

  • Increase AV by 1 for the duration of this Move. Increase EP cost by 1.
  • Add Afflict Burn 3. Increase EP cost by 1.

docs.title: PAS Thermal Engine
docs.text:
The first time you would generate Heat during your Allegiance turn, generate +1 Heat.

Rank 1

  • Increase your maximum Heat cap to 7.
  • Decrease the amount of HP lost to Torch effects by 2.

Rank 2

  • Change "the first time" to "the first two times."
  • Change "your Allegiance turn" to "an Allegiance turn."

Rank 3

  • You can Torch 5 to increase any Move's DMG by 50%, not just Innate Moves.
  • Generate +2 Heat instead.

docs.title: UTL Redline
docs.text:
Cost: 2 FP

Automatically gain Beats equal to 1/2 your Anomaly Tier (rounded up) on a Brawn or Reflex check that involves a burst of physical activity, such as ripping a door off its hinges or leaping a gap.

docs.title: UTL Withstand
docs.text:
Cost: 1 FP

Until the start of the next scene, you may ignore the effects of poisons, drugs, and other toxic substances, as well as severe environmental effects like extreme cold and heat. You cannot fall Unconscious.

docs.title: TRT Unwavering
docs.text: Gain automatic Beats equal to half your Anomaly Tier (rounded up) on all Checks to resist coercion or intimidation.

docs.title: TRT Running Hot
docs.text: You may choose to only rest for 4 hours. If you do, this counts as a full Rest. If you choose to rest a full 8 hours, you heal an additional 6 HP (total 18).

docs.title: Subclasses
docs.text: Unlocking your first move in a subclass locks you into that path.

docs.title: Inferno
docs.text:
Blood boiling in his veins, Gregor surged onwards. Nothing could stop his onslaught.

An Inferno specializes in buffing themselves via self-damage.

docs.title: FEA Flashpoint
docs.text:

docs.title: Bleed
docs.text: At the end of your Allegiance's turn, take True Harm equal to Stacks.

docs.title: Tremor
docs.text: On Break, take True Harm equal to stacks, then clear stacks. While you have at least 1 stack of Tremor, decrease your AV by 2.

docs.title: Shock
docs.text: Whenever you take an Action, take True Harm equal to stacks, then clear stacks. While you have at least 1 stack of Shock, get -1 DIE on all Moves.

docs.title: Burn
docs.text: At the end of your Allegiance's turn, take 2 True Harm. While you have at least 1 stack of Burn, take 2 additional Harm from all sources except Burn.

docs.title: Bloodied
docs.text:
• While Bloodied, you lose 1 Power () on all Moves.
  • While Bloodied, the effectiveness of all healing is halved.
  • Upon healing above half HP or becoming Mortal, remove Bloodied.

docs.title: Mortal
docs.text:
• While Bloodied, you lose 1 Power () on all Moves.
  • While Mortal, the effectiveness of all healing is halved.
  • While Mortal, you lose 3 maximum EP.
  • While Mortal, you cannot be healed other than through surgery, which must be purchased from a doctor.
  • Upon reaching 0 HP or healing above one quarter HP, remove Mortal.

docs.title: Dying
docs.text: Create a Death Clock with three segments. Upon using an Action Slot, taking any amount of Harm, and every hour, roll 1d2. On 1, you mark a segment on the Death Clock. On a 2, you continue to hold onto life. - When you mark your second segment on the Death Clock, you gain the Unconscious Condition until healed to at least 1 HP. - When you mark your third segment on the Death Clock, you die. - Lose Dying upon healing to at least 1 HP.

docs.title: Unconscious
docs.text:
• While Unconscious, you cannot act or react in any way, in or out of combat.
  • Lose Unconscious after 1 hour, or upon taking Harm three times.

docs.title: Hidden
docs.text:
• You cannot be targeted by Moves or abilities that don't have Seeking.
  • Lose Hidden upon using a Move or taking Harm.

docs.title: Disarmed
docs.text:
• You cannot use Moves that don't have Innate.
  • You must expend an Action Slot to clear Disarmed.

docs.title: Afflict X [Status]
docs.text: Apply X stacks of [Status] on Win.

docs.title: Area X
docs.text: Select up to X additional valid targets. Roll for the Clash one time. Each target rolls against your result. Text that says "target" reads "targets" for the purposes of this Move. Area on Offensive Moves only.

docs.title: Breach
docs.text: The target's current Armor Value is halved for this round.

docs.title: Bulwark X
docs.text: Ignore the effects and Harm of up to X Unopposed dice the opponent controls.

docs.title: Deflect
docs.text: If this Move has more Dice than the opposing Move, you are immune to Breaks.

docs.title: Flow X
docs.text: On one or more Wins in a single Clash, gain X EP.

docs.title: Impact
docs.text: If this Move deals 0 Harm, the target loses 1 EP. If the target has no EP, they take 5 true Harm instead.

docs.title: Innate
docs.text: This Move relies on internal power (biology, psionics, code) rather than an external tool. It does not benefit from Weapon Features and does not use Weapon Tier for Scaling. It uses Anomaly Tier for Scaling and can be used while Disarmed.

docs.title: Intercept
docs.text: This Move may be used in response to another Move not targeting you. If you do, you become that Move’s target.

docs.title: Pierce X
docs.text: Ignore X points of the target's AV.

docs.title: Reinforced
docs.text: When you are Broken, only remove the next die in the sequence rather than all subsequent dice.

docs.title: Seeking
docs.text: This Move ignores the Hidden Condition.

docs.title: Vicious
docs.text: If this Move causes a Break, add +10 Harm to the total.

docs.title: 04.9 Wound Table
docs.text:
When you gain a Wound, roll 1d66 and take the result.

Roll Wound Result 11-16 Scar No mechanical impact. You gain a new scar to remember this close call by. 21-23 Staggered Lose 1 Action Slot on your Allegiance's next turn. 24-26 Cracked Ribs Lose 3 EP. If you cannot, take 4 Damage for each EP you would have lost. 31-33 Broken Bone Lose 1 Action Slot for the rest of combat. If this would reduce your Action Slots to 0, instead cap your maximum EP at 4. 34-36 Hemorrhage Gain 18 stacks of Bleed. 41-43 Battle Scar Your Maximum HP is reduced by 20 until this Wound is healed. 44-46 Brain Injury Choose a Class/Subclass Move. It is Disabled until you spend 1 Cred to re-purchase it. If it has upgrades, those upgrades are re-instated upon re-purchasing the Move. 51-53 Gouge Lose an eye. Halve your Reflex (rounded up) and roll -1 DIE on all Moves. 54-56 Sever Roll 1d2.1. Lose Arm: Cap your maximum EP at 3. Cannot manipulate things with two hands (out of combat).2. Lose Leg: Gain Prone. Cannot run (out of combat). 61-65 Critical Strike Reduce HP to 0. Gain Dying. 66 Death Blow Reduce HP to 0. Gain Dying. Instantly mark 2 Death Clock segments.

docs.title: Index
docs.text:
You're stuck here, Kid. No gettin' out. Get used to it. Life here is kill or be killed, eat or be eaten, you know?

Everything in Limbo, from the buildings to the people, is foreign. It all came from a different place. Nothing is native to the City.

The Heart establishes a habitable bubble of space that loops back on itself. The Head establishes the major laws of Limbo, and the Hand enforces them. The Circles create and enforce their own laws. Thus is the way of life in The City.

docs.title: The Recursive Bubble
docs.text: Limbo exists within a recursive bubble, a space that loops back on itself as though it were on the inner plane of a sphere, though there's no visible curvature. If you walked all the way in one direction from the Spine, you'd eventually find yourself arriving on the opposite side of it. Thus, there's no leaving. Those inside are trapped for all eternity.

docs.title: Technology
docs.text: Technology in Limbo is a hodgepodge. From CRT displays to holograms, from sodium lamps to neon lights, technology is a ramshackle mess of a system. The "internet," if one could call it that, is a loose connection of wired networks that span the whole City.

docs.title: The City of Limbo
docs.text: "Generally, the closer one gets to the Heart, the more protection they receive... and the higher one's standard of living becomes."

docs.title: The Bubble
docs.text:
Between the infinite dimensions of the broader universe lies the Void, a space that all interdimensional travelers—from souls on their way to the afterlife to purposeful explorers—must pass through.

Within this Void sits a mysterious construct known as the Heart. The Heart generates a recursive, habitable bubble roughly the size of a medium-sized country, complete with an atmosphere, power, and fresh water. This bubble is a trap: space loops back on itself, so anyone who walks in a straight line away from the Heart will eventually find themselves walking back towards it from the opposite side. Once inside, there is no leaving.

Over time, people and scrap collected in this bubble, forming the sprawling, chaotic metropolis known as The City or Limbo.

docs.title: The Spine and the Heart
docs.text: The City is built around the Spine, a towering monolith of twisted, vine-like white stone that spirals around the Heart, which sits in the precise center of the city. The original creators of the Heart are long gone, though some suspect they still hold power within the city's circles.

docs.title: Zones of Limbo
docs.text: Limbo is a chaotic mess of stacked buildings and ramshackle construction. Its structure is a gradient of wealth and safety, generally split into three sections.

docs.title: The Depths
docs.text: The lowest points of Limbo, a complex maze of decaying buildings, sewers, and shantytowns buried under years of new development. Dark, dangerous, and mostly vacant, few dare to explore these unstable corridors.

docs.title: The Gutters
docs.text: Where the supermajority of the population lives. Conditions range from sketchy to downright dangerous, but inhabitants can at least see the sky between the towering buildings.

docs.title: The Rises
docs.text: The tallest megastructures in the city, typically controlled by single, powerful Circles. This is where everyone wants to be. Even the worst apartments here are safer than the Gutters, and the upper levels are downright lavish.

docs.title: Technology
docs.text:
Limbo's technology is a hodgepodge of the incredibly advanced and the outright antiquated.

  • Advanced: Cloning, cybernetics, teleportation, 3D printing, and holographic imaging are common.
  • Dated: Monochrome holographs, CRTs, and LCDs are the primary displays; LEDs are rare. Sodium lamps are common.
  • Computing: Computers are chunky and slow by modern Earth standards. The "internet" is a loose collection of local wired networks.

docs.title: Artifice and Power
docs.text:
While there is no native "magic" system, many inhabitants bring powers from their original universes. This is known as Artifice and generally cannot be taught or transferred. Other sources of power include:

  • Augmentation: Mechanical cybernetics and biological mutations are common.
  • Artifacts: Powerful objects often fall into Limbo from the Void.

Power Scaling

At low-to-mid power levels, normal guns will kill someone easily. At the highest end, urban legends speak of individuals destroying entire buildings with a single blow.

docs.title: The Head, The Hand, and The Law
docs.text: "Follow those rules, and you'll never see or hear from the Hand."

docs.title: The Head
docs.text:
Limbo is controlled by the Head, a mysterious council of an estimated 5 to 15 individuals who dwell within the Spine. They rarely interfere in the daily lives of the city's inhabitants, save for enforcing three absolute rules.

The Three Rules

  1. Pay your taxes.
  2. Don't interfere with the operations of the Head, Heart, or Hand.
  3. Never use dimensional Artifice within the City.

docs.title: The Hand
docs.text: The Hand is the enforcement arm of the Head. Their primary duties are collecting taxes, registering Circles, and shutting down any power strong enough to threaten the city's stability.

docs.title: The Fingers
docs.text:
The Hand's most terrifying enforcers are the Fingers. Their identities and numbers are secret, but their power is legendary. Facing a single Finger in combat is considered suicidal for even the most powerful individuals. They average around Anomaly Tier VII.

The Eclipse Hour

In an event known as the Eclipse Hour, four Fingers massacred the entire leadership of Orion, a powerful Circle, in just under three hours.

docs.title: Auditors
docs.text: When a Finger is overkill, the Hand sends an Auditor. Rumored to be lab-grown, these humanoid creatures are tall and imposing, averaging around seven feet. They wear pristine white armored trench coats and spherical, black fishbowl helmets with a ring of yellow holographic caution tape encircling it. They fight with a pair of black gauntlets that can emit bursts of raw energy and enhance the Auditor's physical strength. Auditors serve as the main tax collectors in Limbo, and are all Anomaly Tier IV. They're dangerous, but can be brought down by coordinated groups of Tier II and III individuals. The threat, however, isn't the Auditor itself -- it's the infinite number of them the Hand will send, and eventually, the escalation to a Finger.

docs.title: Circles
docs.text:
Circles are groups officially recognized by the Hand. They control almost every aspect of day-to-day life in the otherwise lawless city, creating a complex web of tyranny and chaos.

Circles can be anything from businesses to crime syndicates to cults. Registration is mandatory for tax purposes, though many try to avoid it.

docs.title: Notable Circles
docs.text: Circle Name Description The Ashen Brotherhood One of the largest smuggling rings, trading in slaves and drugs from the Depths. Ironically, they are tax-compliant. The Red Guard Rowdy, disorganized soldiers made up of the impoverished, serving the Red Queen. The Iron Legion A paramilitary group of heavily augmented mercenary super-soldiers. The Network Information peddlers who deal in rumors, blackmail, and news. They run the trusted Blue Gazette The Chitter A group that has genetically modified themselves into an insect-like hive mind. The False Heart False prophets who seek to build a "second Heart" out of flesh and wicked magic to consume the city. The Whispering Way Anonymous, steam-augmented alchemists and mages who worship the Spine and Heart as a god. The Infinite Ones Mages experimenting with time distortion, led by a mysterious dimension walker. The Burning Crazed individuals who use Artifice and cybernetics to burn their souls as fuel for superhuman feats. The Light Stealers A deranged offshoot of The Burning obsessed with achieving enough speed to break the recursive bubble. The Skyhold Consortium A massive construction corporation that builds the Rises and deals in robotics and defense systems. The Coalition of Infinite War (CIW) A Circle that hires lost weapon makers to create unique, powerful weapons for the city's interdimensional inhabitants.

docs.title: The Grind
docs.text: For most people, life in Limbo is an endless, mundane grind for a local Circle. Violence is common, but if you keep your head down and eyes forward, you'll likely live to see tomorrow. Unfortunately, you're not most people.

docs.title: The Inhabitants
docs.text: While many disparate and unusual creatures exist within Limbo, the city is defined by its sapient population. In local parlance, "human" is a specific classification, but all sapients are referred to as "people," provided they possess intelligence and free will.

docs.title: Humans
docs.text:
Humans are the most populous demographic in the City. They are not strictly Homo Sapiens Sapiens, but rather a collection of highly similar hominids capable of interbreeding.

While some may possess extra limbs, redundant organs, strange skin tones, or other anomalies, they are reliably found in all parts of the City. Most are born in Limbo rather than trapped there, having been part of the City's ecology for as long as it has existed.

However, other, stranger beings exist, such as the floating, psychic, intelligent jellyfish-like creatures known as Nidians.

docs.title: Dimension Walkers
docs.text:
Most people who enter Limbo are trapped permanently by the recursive bubble. However, one notable exception exists: Dimension Walkers.

The Walkers

These are rare beings capable of circumventing the bubble and leaving Limbo. They are typically solitary figures, unaligned in major conflicts, who appear sporadically to peddle new technologies, information, or goods from the outside world.

docs.title: Ecology and Diet
docs.text: Food in Limbo is defined by scarcity and the lack of sunlight. The cramped interior farms of the City mostly grow plants that thrive in the dark.

docs.title: Staples
docs.text:
• Crops: Potatoes, mushrooms, and lichen are the foundation of the Limbo diet.
  • Flour: Wheat is inefficient to grow and rare. Most "flour" is supplemented with Sump Root, a starchy cave vine with high amylopectin content used for flatbreads and doughs.
  • Protein: Real meat is a luxury often imported by Dimension Walkers. The average citizen relies on insects and the eggs of the Krezz (a ubiquitous arachnid the size of a large rat).

The Voidfish

Rarely, in contaminated streams and deep arroyos, one can find the Voidfish, a translucent bottom-feeder with tender, fragile flesh filled with tiny bones.

docs.title: Luxuries and Vices
docs.text:
• Water & Salt: The Heart produces fresh water, but it has an extremely low sodium content. By reducing it massively, one can harvest Salt. Salt is so expensive it functions as a currency standard, backing the main currency of Limbo, the Copper Sal.
  • Sugar: Obtained by chemically breaking down potato starch (creating a cheap, bitter sweetener) or reducing Sump Root sap.
  • Alcohol & Stimulants: Beer and hard liquor are typically brewed from potatoes. Cigarettes are made from a mildly hallucinogenic mushroom that instills slight euphoria. A caffeinated algae steeped in water takes the place of tea or coffee.

docs.title: Seasonings
docs.text: Spices are used to mask the taste of rot or bland starch. - Bitterroot: Powdered and used in poorer areas. - Redbloom: A sour, parasitic flowering plant that feeds on lichen. - Fedder: A type of algae that smells complex and herbaceous when dried. - Oil: Rendered from Krezz corpses or the parasitic Oilvine.

docs.title: NPC Creation
docs.text: NPCs in Limbo are simplified reflections of Player Characters. They use the same physics (Clashing, EP, Action Slots), but they are built to be run efficiently by the GM.

docs.title: The Chassis: Tier & Role
docs.text: To create an NPC, first determine their Anomaly Tier (AT) and their Role. These two factors dictate their raw stats.

docs.title: Step A: Base Stats by Tier
docs.text:
Use this table to set the baseline.

Tier Base HP Action Slots Attribute Range I 10 1 0 - 2 II 20 1 1 - 3 III 40 2 2 - 4 IV 60 2 3 - 5 V 80 2 4 - 6 VI 100 3 5 - 7 VII+ 150+ 3+ 8 - 10

docs.title: Step B: The Role Modifier
docs.text:
Apply these modifiers to the Base Stats above.

Role Description HP Modifier Repertoire Limit Minion Fodder, swarms, civilians. 0.5x 1 Off / 1 Def Elite Professional soldiers, horrors. 1.0x 2 Off / 2 Def / 1 Passive Boss Multi-Part entities (See Section 3). Sum of Parts Custom (Per Part)

docs.title: The Repertoire
docs.text: NPCs do not swap Moves. They have a static list. * Weapons: If an NPC carries a weapon, give them 1-2 Standard Moves from that Shell. * Custom Moves: Elites and Bosses should have unique Moves.

docs.title: Complex Entities (Multi-Part Bosses)
docs.text: High-threat entities (usually 1 to 2 Tiers above the party) are too complex to be represented by a single health bar. To balance the Action Economy against a full party, these entities are composed of multiple Parts.

docs.title: Anatomy of a Boss
docs.text:
A Boss consists of a Core and 1 or more Parts (Limbs, Weapons, Familiars).

  • Action Slots: The Boss typically has 3 to 4 Action Slots, regardless of how many Parts it has or its Tier. Any Part can use an Action Slot.
  • Shared EP: The Boss has a single EP pool (Max 6). Any Part can generate or spend EP.
  • Status Effects: Status Effects apply to the each Part individually, whether they're applied to a Part or the Core. If a Part gets a stack of Shock then its associated Moves get -1 DIE, but no other Moves do. Shock triggers only when that Part uses a Move.

docs.title: Damage and Targeting
docs.text:
Players may target the Core or a specific Part.

  1. Damage Transfer: Any Harm dealt to a Part is subtracted from that Part's HP, and half that Harm is dealt to the Core. If a Part takes 12 Harm, the Core takes 6.
  2. Part Destruction: When a Part reaches 0 HP, it is Destroyed. All its associated Moves are inaccessible, and any carryover Harm is dealt directly to the Core.

docs.title: Consequences of Destruction
docs.text:
When a Part is Destroyed, the following rules apply immediately:

  1. Loss of Moves: The Boss can no longer use Moves associated with that Part.
  2. Desperation (Optional): If specified in the Stat Block, the Core may unlock a "Desperation Move" or gain a buff (e.g., Enraged) to compensate for the lost limb.
Example: The Scrap-Titan (Tier V Boss)
  • Total HP: 240. Actions: 4.
  • Core (Torso): 160 HP. Moves: Vent Heat, Barricade.
  • Part A (Saw Arm): 40 HP. Moves: Buzz Saw.
  • Part B (Clamp Arm): 40 HP. Moves: Crush.

Combat Flow: 1. Players target the Saw Arm and deal 40 Damage. 2. The Saw Arm is Destroyed. 3. The Core takes 40 Damage. 4. The Titan loses the Buzz Saw move.

docs.title: Loot Rules
docs.text: In Limbo, equipment is physical. If an enemy uses it, it is on their body.

docs.title: The Drop Rule
docs.text:
• Weapons: If an NPC attacks with a Long Gun, they drop a Long Gun Shell (Tier appropriate).
  • Armor: Armor is usually destroyed during the fight (reduced to Scrap).

docs.title: The Lock Rule
docs.text:
To prevent players from looting game-breaking equipment from Bosses, use these tags:

  • Bio-Locked: The weapon's trigger is coded to the user's DNA. It is useless scrap to anyone else.
  • Artifice-Bound: The weapon is a manifestation of the user's will. It dissolves into smoke upon their death.
  • Volatile: The power source is unstable. The item explodes or melts when the user dies.

docs.title: Flattening (Demoting Bosses)
docs.text:
You may wish to reuse a Boss model as a "Elite" enemy for higher-tier players later in the campaign. To do this, Flatten the stat block.

  1. Merge HP: Combine the HP of the Parts into a single pool (usually lowered to the standard Elite multiplier for that Tier).
  2. Merge Repertoire: Move the Part-specific moves into the main list.
  3. Remove Parts: The entity becomes a single target.
